title: Under-Loft-Bed Tall Bookcase
summary: A tall 1x12 bookcase sized to slot under a loft bed in place of its leg support, turning wasted under-bed floor into real shelf storage.
description:
The whole point of a loft bed is the floor space it frees up underneath, so a bookcase that just sits there is only half the idea — this one is built to actually replace one of the loft bed's own leg supports, screwed straight to the bed's legs to keep them square. Build the loft bed itself with that support left out on the side this bookcase goes, since the bookcase takes over that structural job.

The top two shelves carry their load through end grain rather than through the back panel like the bottom shelf does, which is a weaker joint under real weight — scrap 1x2 supports screwed under those shelves, hidden by the shelf trim, close that gap for next to nothing since the material is already left over from the trim cuts.

tools.note: Optional — pocket holes under the shelves are cleaner than face screws, but not required.

materials.name: Pine, 1x12, 10 ft
materials.note: One yields the two sides, the other yields all five shelves.

materials.name: Plywood, 1/4", 30" x 38" or larger, for back

cutList.note: Scrap from the shelf trim cuts; supports the two top shelves under their end-grain joints.

steps.title: Cut the case parts
steps.body: Cut two sides 56" long and five shelves 36" long from the 1x12, one footer 36" from the 1x3, and four shelf trim pieces 36" from the 1x2; save the trim offcuts for the four optional 9" shelf supports. Every 1x12 part is the board's full 11-1/4" width, so only the lengths change and no ripping is needed. Check the board count before you shop: the two 56" sides fit on one 10 ft (120") 1x12 with about 7-7/8" left, but five 36" shelves do not fit on a single 10 ft board — three fit at 108-1/4" with the kerf and the last two need a second board, so this case takes three 10 ft 1x12, not the two the material list shows. All four 36" trim pieces and all four 9" supports come out of two 8 ft 1x2. Square every cut and label the pieces as they come off the saw.

steps.title: Build the loft bed with one support left out
steps.body: Build the companion loft bed first, but leave out the bottom leg support on the side where this bookcase will be installed — the bookcase takes over that structural job. This bookcase is sized for a twin loft bed; for a full-size bed, cut every shelf and trim piece to (measure from the mattress) instead of 36".

steps.title: Build the box
steps.body: The two 56" sides run the full height and the five 36" shelves fit between them, which makes the finished case 37-1/2" wide (36" of shelf plus 3/4" of side at each end) and 56" tall. The plan's own footer step confirms that reading, since the 36" 1x3 footer is pocket-screwed through its ends into the sides. Mark every shelf position on both sides before you assemble anything, drawing the line on both faces of each joint so you can see where the board lands and where the fastener goes. With the pocket-hole jig, drill 3/4"-setting pocket holes into the undersides of the shelves and drive 2" screws with glue into the sides; without it, screw through the outside face of each side into the shelf ends, with glue either way. Set the top and bottom shelves flush with the ends of the sides and space the middle three evenly — with 3/4" stock that leaves roughly 13" of clear height per bay — and check that both diagonals match, because the back panel and shelf trim will lock in whatever shape you leave.

steps.title: Attach the footer
steps.body: Attach the footer to the bottom of the case. With a pocket hole jig, drill pocket holes in the ends of the 1x3 and attach to the sides; otherwise use screws and glue the same way as the box.

steps.title: Attach the back
steps.body: Measure the assembled case across its outside corners and cut the 1/4" plywood back to that size — the material line calls for a 30" x 38" piece, which is smaller than the 37-1/2" x 56" case it has to cover, so buy the back to your own assembled dimensions rather than to that number. Square the case first: measure both diagonals, rack it until they match, and nail the back on while it is holding that shape. Attach with glue and 3/4" to 1-1/4" brad nails, catching every shelf as well as both sides — nailing into the shelves is what stops the case racking under load, so a perimeter-only nailing pattern wastes the panel's whole structural job.
materials: Plywood, 1/4", 30" x 38" or larger, for back

steps.title: Attach the top shelf trim
steps.body: Attach the top shelf trim the same way as the footer, with glue and 2" screws.

steps.title: Add the optional shelf supports
steps.body: If the top two shelves will see real weight, cut scrap 1x2 pieces and fasten them to the sides just under those shelves — they're attached through end grain rather than the back panel like the bottom shelf, so this support adds real strength for the cost of material you already have leftover from the trim cuts.

steps.title: Install under the loft bed
steps.body: Screw the bookcase to the sides of the loft bed's legs to stiffen the bed — the bookcase replaces the missing bottom support and is what keeps those legs square.

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand every surface smooth and finish to match the loft bed.

images.alt: Tall bookcase built to slot under a loft bed
